final_answer: 1. Reproduce the checkout flow in a controlled environment and record request/response timestamps together with DB logs (N1:0:1931d796). 2. Temporarily disable the new join via a feature flag and benchmark latency to isolate its impact (N7:6:8b7d7b5a). 3. Capture the exact SQL of the checkout query and run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) to identify full scans or missing indexes (N2:1:ac3867da, N6:1:69b9e8ca). 4. Verify that all join columns have appropriate B‑tree indexes and add any missing ones (N2:2:75622d44). 5. Run load‑test scenarios that mimic the increased client retries to observe latency distribution and error rates (N6:6:26f453df). 6. Define a timeout budget: set client retry timeout to observed p99 latency plus network RTT plus a safety margin (≈20%) (N10:2:2f8ab628). 7. Adjust client retry logic to use exponential back‑off and retry only on network‑level failures, limiting retries to two attempts (N5:5:5c00c39b). 8. Wrap each payment request in a transaction that includes a unique idempotency key, and enforce a unique constraint on (order_id, payment_attempt) to make inserts idempotent (N1:3:631ac693, N6:4:949a31fe). 9. Deploy a circuit breaker around the payment service to stop further retries when latency spikes, preventing overload (implicit from best practice). 10. After fixes, re‑enable the join, monitor latency percentiles and duplicate‑charge alerts, and roll back if regressions appear (N6:6:26f453df).
